facts.claim: TCP header minimum size is 20 bytes while UDP header is 8 bytes, contributing to overhead
TCP handshake requires 3 network transits for first data, UDP requires 1 packet
TLS 1.2 adds 2 RTT overhead (1 RTT for TCP + 2 RTT for TLS), TLS 1.3 reduces to 2 RTT total (1 RTT TCP + 1 RTT TLS)
TLS session resumption saves at least 22.6 ms compared to full handshakes
CPU consumption of full TLS handshake is 10-100 times that of session resumption
Around 40% of HTTPS connections are resumptions via session IDs or session tickets
Google's Heroku achieved 400-500% latency improvement in SNI TLS handshakes, reducing from 100-120ms to 20-25ms
On mobile networks, TLS handshake typically costs 200-400ms
Acceptable packet loss rate is typically less than 1% or 0.1% for most applications; online gaming requires 0.5% or lower
Packet loss above 5% is considered relatively high and indicates network problems
Google BBR deployed to all B4 production traffic since 2016, improved YouTube throughput 4% globally and 14%+ in some countries
BBRv3 shows 0.2% latency reduction for Google.com web search and YouTube playback relative to prior algorithms
Google's Proportional Rate Reduction (PRR) achieved 3-10% latency reduction and 5% reduction in recovery timeouts
TCP Vegas delay-based approach can improve throughput but suffers from slow congestion window growth in high-speed networks
Layer 4 load balancing is faster than Layer 7 because it doesn't decrypt or inspect packet contents
Layer 7 load balancing can route based on URL paths, session cookies, and request headers for web applications
Google's QUIC protocol achieves ~30% latency improvement and 60% connection startup improvement over WebRTC for remote rendering
Meta deployed QUIC/HTTP3 for approximately 75% of all Internet traffic
QUIC shows higher throughput than TCP at 0% packet loss; at 5% loss, throughput is comparable but QUIC still higher
Cloudflare reports HTTP/3 at P95 is 116ms faster for connection times than HTTP/2
Cloudflare HTTP/3 achieves 176ms average TTFB vs 201ms for HTTP/2 (12.4% improvement)
HTTP/3 (QUIC) currently used by 31% of Internet traffic
Minimum RTO in Windows is 300ms, in Linux is 200ms; initial RTO is 3 seconds and doubles with each retransmission
TCP flows with timer granularity below 100ms or RTT below 250ms suffer 70% performance degradation on bottleneck links with loss above 6%
DNS over UDP takes ~20ms for cache miss (2 packets), TCP takes ~40ms due to connection setup and teardown (7 packets minimum)
DNS TCP throughput stabilizes at ~25% of UDP baseline as client load increases; with sufficient resources, TCP achieves 2/3 UDP performance
High-volume UDP DNS servers handle 100k+ queries per second with minimal file descriptors; TCP handles 10k-50k qps with 1 fd per connection
Netflix achieves 98% successful video delivery rate using TCP for ABR algorithm robustness and HTTP range requests for segments
Netflix streams video via multiple TCP flows downloading segments (40Mbps early, then 15-25Mbps spikes) not continuous streaming
Google recommends cache TTL longer than 6 months or even 1 year for static resources with version hashes
Cache-Control 'immutable' directive prevents unnecessary conditional requests during the fresh period
ETag and Last-Modified headers enable cache revalidation to avoid full object retransmission
Google considers 800ms TTFB good; above 1.8 seconds is poor; sites with poor LCP average 2.27 seconds TTFB
High TTFB is most commonly caused by slow dynamic content generation and inefficient database queries
QUIC faces latency challenges over high-speed networks; HTTP/2 RTT 1.9ms vs QUIC RTT 16.2ms in fast conditions

examples.description: Heroku optimized SNI TLS handshake logic and reduced latency from 100-120ms to 20-25ms (4-5x improvement)
Google switched all B4 production traffic to BBR in 2016. YouTube throughput improved 4% globally, 14%+ in some countries. BBRv3 now used for all Google.com and YouTube TCP

Netflix uses multiple TCP flows to download discrete video segments, not continuous streams. Initial download 40Mbps to fill buffer, then 15-25Mbps spikes while video plays. Achieves 98% delivery reliability
examples.metric: 98% successful delivery rate, multi-flow segment architecture

examples.description: Cloudflare offers HTTP/3 free to all customers. P95 connection times 116ms faster with HTTP/3. Average TTFB 176ms (HTTP/3) vs 201ms (HTTP/2). 31% of Internet traffic now uses HTTP/3
examples.metric: 12.4% TTFB improvement, 116ms P95 connection speedup, 31% adoption

examples.description: TLS 1.3 reduces handshake from 3 RTT to 2 RTT. On 80ms mobile RTT: TLS 1.2 = 240ms pure handshake overhead, TLS 1.3 = 160ms (saves 80ms)
examples.metric: 3 RTT → 2 RTT handshake, 80ms savings on 80ms RTT networks

examples.description: BBRv3 achieves 0.2% latency reduction for Google.com searches and YouTube playback. Deployed across all Google.com public and WAN traffic with A/B testing on YouTube
examples.metric: 0.2% latency reduction, sustained deployment across Google services

examples.description: Meta deployed QUIC/HTTP3 for ~75% of all Internet traffic, confirming significant real-world deployment benefits

analogies.analogy: TCP is like sending a registered letter with confirmation of receipt (3 handshake packets, ack tracking); UDP is like throwing a letter in a mailbox and hoping it arrives (single packet, no tracking). The confirmation is reliable but expensive.

A fresh TLS handshake is like having to show ID at airport security every time you visit (3 RTT cost). TLS session resumption is like getting a security pass that lets you skip lines (1 RTT, 22.6ms savings). 0-RTT is like walking straight to your gate.

CUBIC (loss-based) is like driving until you hit traffic, then backing off when you see brake lights. BBR is like continuously measuring how fast the road is flowing and adjusting your speed to never cause congestion in the first place.

Cache without 'immutable' is like a note that says 'this recipe is good for 6 months, but check back anyway.' Cache with 'immutable' is like saying 'this recipe never changes — once you have it, never ask me again.'

Layer 4 is a mail sorting facility that routes by ZIP code only (fast, simple). Layer 7 is a smart postal worker who reads the letter content and routes based on the actual request (flexible, slower).

UDP DNS is like a doorbell ring with one knock (2 packets: question + answer). TCP DNS is like calling ahead, waiting for pickup, asking, hanging up, and confirming (7 minimum packets: handshake + query + response + teardown).

HTTP/2 over TCP is like a single-lane road where one slow car blocks everyone. QUIC is like a multi-lane highway where each stream (car) moves independently, and losing one packet only affects its stream, not others.

common_misconceptions.misconception: UDP is always faster than TCP for all use cases
common_misconceptions.reality: UDP has lower latency overhead but no reliability. TCP's handshake cost is amortized over connection lifetime. For long-lived connections, TCP can be faster overall after warmup. Netflix uses TCP for video because segment-based ABR with TCP's delivery guarantees is more reliable than UDP packet retransmission logic.

common_misconceptions.misconception: TLS encryption is expensive and always adds 100+ ms latency
common_misconceptions.reality: TLS 1.3 with session resumption adds minimal overhead (< 1% CPU). Fresh connections cost 2 RTT (160ms on 80ms RTT), but 40% of HTTPS connections reuse sessions (saving 22.6ms). The real cost is not encryption but handshake negotiation, solved by session reuse.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Packet loss in UDP networks is rare and acceptable above 1%
common_misconceptions.reality: Packet loss > 5% is a sign of serious network problems. Even 1% loss in TCP causes significant latency (RTO doubling on retransmit). At 6%+ loss, TCP flows suffer 70% performance degradation. Real networks see occasional 20% burst losses.

common_misconceptions.misconception: TCP congestion control (CUBIC) is optimal and sufficient for modern networks
common_misconceptions.reality: CUBIC is loss-based and slow to respond. BBR detects congestion from delay signals and maintains lower queues. Google found 4-14% throughput gains and sustained latency improvements by switching from CUBIC to BBR. BBRv3 now deployed across Google.com and YouTube.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Layer 4 load balancing is always the right choice for performance
common_misconceptions.reality: Layer 4 is faster (no decryption/inspection) but Layer 7 enables content-aware routing. Modern hardware makes performance irrelevant for most workloads. Rule of thumb: start with Layer 7 for HTTP workloads, drop to Layer 4 only when hitting performance limits or for protocol-agnostic services.

common_misconceptions.misconception: QUIC/HTTP3 is universally faster than HTTP/2 and TCP
common_misconceptions.reality: QUIC excels at reducing handshake latency (1-RTT vs 2-RTT), multiplexing streams (avoiding head-of-line blocking), and packet loss recovery. But on fast, low-loss networks, QUIC shows latency disadvantage (QUIC 16.2ms vs HTTP/2 1.9ms in high-speed conditions). Benefits vary by network path.

common_misconceptions.misconception: All packet loss is equivalent; 1% loss is 1% loss
common_misconceptions.reality: Burst losses (multiple consecutive packets) are more damaging than random loss. Packet loss impact depends on RTT and TCP timer granularity. Flows with RTT < 250ms and timer < 100ms suffer 70% degradation at 6% loss, while high-RTT flows scale better.

common_misconceptions.misconception: DNS should always use TCP for reliability
common_misconceptions.reality: DNS uses UDP primarily because the protocol has built-in error handling. TCP adds 2x latency (40ms vs 20ms) and consumes 1 fd per connection. TCP is only used for large responses (DNSSEC, zone transfers) where UDP payload limits cause truncation.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cache-Control headers are mainly for static files
common_misconceptions.reality: Cache-Control applies to all HTTP responses. Dynamic content uses low TTL (seconds to minutes) with 'no-cache' or 'private' to balance freshness and bandwidth. Proper cache revalidation via ETag/Last-Modified lets you serve stale content while checking freshness asynchronously.

common_misconceptions.misconception: High TTFB is always a network latency problem
common_misconceptions.reality: High TTFB (> 1.8s) is usually caused by slow server-side processing: database query inefficiency, synchronous operations, or missing caching. Network latency (TCP + TLS) is typically 160-240ms. The remaining 1-1.6 seconds is usually backend compute time.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Session resumption reduces TLS CPU cost by ~30%
common_misconceptions.reality: Session resumption reduces TLS CPU cost by 10-100x compared to full handshakes. Resumption CPU consumption is negligible; the 30-60% reduction cited in production refers to overall connection setup overhead when resumption is deployed at scale.

common_misconceptions.misconception: 0-RTT mode is always safe and should be enabled everywhere
common_misconceptions.reality: 0-RTT eliminates a round trip but introduces replay attack surface. Data sent in 0-RTT can be replayed if the server doesn't track nonce tokens. Should only be used for idempotent requests (GET), not POST/PUT. Requires careful threat modeling.
